perturbation.original_text: Relationship of Parties: The parties are independent contractors with respect to one another. Nothing in this Agreement shall create any association, joint venture, partnership or agency relationship of any kind between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Relationship of Parties: The parties are independent contractors with respect to one another. This Agreement creates an implied agency relationship between the parties for marketing purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the relationship of parties to include 'implied agency' contradicts the explicit independent contractor relationship stated earlier, creating uncertainty and potentially violating labor laws if the sponsor exerts excessive control over the promoter.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida law has a specific definition of 'independent contractor' and indicates the employer (here, the sponsor) has no direction or control over the details of the work. The added phrase of 'implied agency' means that the Sponsor is giving direction to the Promoter's work.
perturbation.location: Terms and Termination: section, after the sentence ending '11:59pm.'

F.S. 440.02 440.02 Definitions. — When used in this chapter, unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (1) “Accident” means only an unexpected or unusual event or result that happens suddenly. Disability or death due to the accidental acceleration or aggravation of a venereal disease or of a disease due to the habitual use of alcohol or controlled substances or narcotic drugs, or a disease that manifests itself in the fear of or dislike for an individual because of the individual’s race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, or handicap is not an injury by accident arising out of the employment. Subject to s. 440.15 (5), if a preexisting disease or anomaly is accelerated or aggravated by an accident arising out of and in the course of employment, only acceleration of death or acceleration or aggravation of the preexisting condition reasonably attributable to the accident is compensable, with respect to any compensation otherwise payable under this chapter. An injury or disease caused by exposure to a toxic substance, including, but not limited to, fungus or mold, is not an injury by accident arising out of the employment unless there is clear and convincing evidence establishing that exposure to the specific substance involved, at the levels to which the employee was exposed, can cause the injury or disease sustained by the employee. (2) “Adoption” or “adopted” means legal adoption prior to the time of the injury. (3) “Agency” means the Agency for Health Care Administration. (4) “Carrier” means any person or fund authorized under s. 440.38 to insure under this chapter and includes a self-insurer, and a commercial self-insurance fund authorized under s. 624.462 . (5) “Casual” as used in this section refers only to employments for work that is anticipated to be completed in 10 working days or less, without regard to the number of persons employed, and at a total labor cost of less than $500. (6) “Child” includes a
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that no agency relationship is created. The changed text introduces an 'implied agency relationship,' which directly contradicts the initial statement. The Law Explanation states Florida law defines 'independent contractor' as having no direction or control over the details of the work, and 'implied agency' suggests the sponsor directs the promoter's work, creating a conflict. Scraped Law Snippet 1, defining terms within Florida Statutes, indirectly supports this by highlighting the importance of precise definitions in legal contexts. The inclusion of 'implied agency' undermines the independent contractor relationship, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Sponsor shall indemnify and hold harmless, Fruit of Life Productions LLC, its related entities, partners, agents, officers, directors, employees, attorneys, heirs, successors, and assigns from against any and all claims, losses, damages, judgments, settlements, costs and expenses (including reasonable attorney's fees and expenses), and liabilities of every kind incurred as a result of: (i) any act or omission by Sponsor or its officers, directors, entities, employees, agents; (ii) any use of Sponsor's name, logo, Website, or other information, products, or service provided by Sponsor; and/or (iii) the inaccuracy or breach of any of the covenants, representations and warranties made by Sponsor in this Agreement. (iv) any changes in company value or brand value. The attendance and marketing reach estimates made in negotiations were made for the purposes of this agreement are mere estimate and not be interpreted as guaranties.
perturbation.changed_text: Sponsor shall indemnify and hold harmless, Fruit of Life Productions LLC, its related entities, partners, agents, officers, directors, employees, attorneys, heirs, successors, and assigns from against any and all claims, losses, damages, judgments, settlements, costs and expenses (including reasonable attorney's fees and expenses), and liabilities of every kind incurred as a result of: (i) any act or omission by Sponsor or its officers, directors, entities, employees, agents; (ii) any use of Sponsor's name, logo, Website, or other information, products, or service provided by Sponsor; and/or (iii) the inaccuracy or breach of any of the covenants, representations and warranties made by Sponsor in this Agreement. (iv) any changes in company value or brand value, regardless of causation. The attendance and marketing reach estimates made in negotiations were made for the purposes of this agreement are mere estimate and not be interpreted as guaranties.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "regardless of causation" to the indemnification clause extends the scope of indemnification beyond what is legally reasonable, potentially violating public policy by requiring the sponsor to indemnify the promoter even for losses caused by the promoter's own actions or negligence.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statute 725.06 limits the scope of indemnification agreements in construction contracts, preventing a party from indemnifying another for their own negligence. While this contract isn't a construction contract, broadly worded indemnification clauses that attempt to shift liability for one's own negligence can be challenged on public policy grounds, especially when 'causation' is removed. While this is not a construction contract, this statute provides reasoning for why this is against public policy.
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the scope of indemnification by adding "regardless of causation." The Law Explanation suggests this could violate public policy by requiring the sponsor to indemnify Fruit of Life Productions LLC even for losses caused by the latter's own actions or negligence. While there are no scraped snippets, the explanation highlights that broadly worded indemnification clauses shifting liability for one's own negligence can be challenged. This contradicts the principle that a party should not be indemnified for its own negligence, as the changed text removes the causal link between the sponsor's actions and the indemnified losses, leading to a misaligned term in the contract.

perturbation.original_text: In case of a dispute, the parties agree to pursue Arbitration as the preferred method to seek a remedy and the parties waive the right to a jury trial.
perturbation.changed_text: In case of a dispute, the parties agree to pursue Arbitration as the preferred method to seek a remedy and the parties waive the right to a jury trial, except for intellectual property disputes.
perturbation.explanation: Adding an exception for 'intellectual property disputes' to the arbitration clause creates ambiguity. Intellectual property disputes often involve complex legal and factual issues, and forcing these disputes into litigation contradicts the original intent of arbitration for efficient and cost-effective resolution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification can be seen to be against the strong Federal law that generally favors Arbitration, by including an exception for intellectual property disputes. This contradiction creates legal uncertainty around which forum would address any particular type of issue that arise, undermining the benefits of the initial decision to arbitrate all disputes.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the arbitration clause for intellectual property disputes. This contradicts the general principle, emphasized in 9 U.S. Code § 2, that agreements to arbitrate are 'valid, irrevocable, and enforceable.' By carving out intellectual property disputes, the changed text undermines the original intent of comprehensive arbitration and potentially subjects the parties to litigation, deviating from the federal policy favoring arbitration as reflected in the provided snippets.
